claims.text: The willingness to pay for a identical product varies dramatically based on context of purchase and delivery format. Coca-Cola sold in a refrigerated can from a vending machine commands 6-8 times the unit-volume price of the same product at room temperature in a two-liter bottle from a discount store.
claims.speaker: Unknown speaker
conditions: purchase context varies (vending machine vs discount retail)
claims.mechanism: The context and delivery format establish perceived scarcity and convenience, justifying premium pricing.

claims.text: Packaging is a major component of the overall value proposition and directly determines the customer experience, influencing purchasing decisions. The same product in different packaging (bottle vs can for water) elicits different willingness-to-purchase responses.
claims.speaker: Unknown speaker
claims.mechanism: Packaging creates a behavioral pairing that conditions purchasing behavior; the visual and tactile format becomes part of the product experience itself.

examples.text: A consumer will refuse to purchase water from a bottle (rejecting the proposition) but changes their response when the same water is presented in a can, accepting it as a desirable part of their hydration routine. This demonstrates how packaging alone shifts perceived value despite identical product.

Coca-Cola availability in 12 different purchase contexts (refrigerated vending machine single-serve vs room-temperature bulk discount) results in a 6-8x price variance for the identical beverage, illustrating how context of purchase determines value perception.
